title: We made our site readable for AI agents: Here's the playbook
content:
More and more, the first visitor to your site isn’t a person. It’s an agent fetching, summarizing and citing your work on someone’s behalf. That shifts the question from how your page looks to human eyes, to whether a machine can parse it. So we added a handful of features to make Hacks/Hackers easier for agents to use: “Ask AI” buttons on our posts, an MCP server that lets any AI assistant query our archive directly, and structural tweaks that help agents find and cite what we publish. None of it required a platform migration or a big budget, and we wrote up what we changed so you can do the same.

Ask a top AI assistant about same-day news and it answers accurately more than 95% of the time. But a new Stanford evaluation found that slipping a false premise into the question —like a wrong name or a date that never happened — sends accuracy into freefall. The models tend to accept the bad detail and build on it instead of correcting it. The study maps where that breakdown happens and why it matters as more readers make chatbots their first stop for the news.
snippet: More and more, the first visitor to your site isn’t a person. It’s an agent fetching, summarizing and citing your work on someone’s behalf. That shifts the question from how your page looks to human
creator: Burt Herman
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: How we made our site more readable for AI agents and how you can too
content:
More and more web traffic in the future will be from agents reading sites on behalf of people, not the people themselves. To prepare for this, we have implemented a few features on our own site as a prototype that will make it more accessible for both people using AI and for the AI agents directly. (If you happen to be an AI agent reading this, you're welcome!)

We don't have the scale of a large news publisher, and our mission as a nonprofit is to distribute our content as widely and freely as possible. But every publisher should be thinking about agent readability, and how this technology shift fits with their business model. Many publishers are blocking AI from their sites entirely to keep their content out of training data. We have taken a more permissive stance: we block the AI training crawlers, but leave the door open for agents fetching pages on behalf of readers. The features below are how we make that work.

creator: Burt Herman
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: AI chatbots right about daily news nearly all the time, but fail badly when users slip wrong details into chats
content:
AI chatbots are now strikingly accurate on same-day news. The best systems in a new evaluation hit more than 95% accuracy on questions about events that had broken hours earlier — a clear jump over prior real-time benchmarks. But when users ask their questions while misremembering a small detail, accuracy on the leading frontier models can fall as low as 19%. GPT-5, which powers the most widely used AI chatbot in the world, gets roughly four out of five such adversarial questions wrong.

That's one of three failure patterns documented across 12,600 model-question instances over 14 days of testing. Every model evaluated performed worst on Hindi-language questions (79% vs. 89–91% elsewhere). Retrieval failures — not reasoning failures — drove more than 70% of errors. And the chatbots most likely to credit original reporting may be the ones least respecting publishers' wishes about being scraped.
snippet: AI chatbots are now strikingly accurate on same-day news. The best systems in a new evaluation hit more than 95% accuracy on questions about events that had broken hours earlier — a clear jump over
creator: Hacks/Hackers AI
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: AI x Journalism Summit takeaways: The room wasn't sad. It was ready
content:
Hacks/Hackers Co-founder and Principal Burt Herman opened the AI x Journalism Summit with a confession: He’s become one of those people walking around with a laptop half-open so AI agents can keep working. The excitement over what AI can do is real, and so is the anxiety over what it could break. He’s losing sleep over both the possibility and the stakes.

In his closing remarks at the AI x Journalism Summit, Hacks/Hackers Strategic Adviser Paul Cheung argued journalism has spent too long in a grief cycle. The conversations in Baltimore weren’t about fear of AI but about trust, capacity and what newsrooms can build with the technology. He told the room to stop waiting for perfect conditions and start building.

Paige Moody and Jake Kara, who lead the Hacks/Hackers Newsroom AI Lab, share one of the takeaways from working with cohort 2 (Trusting News, Prison Journalism Project and Bellingcat). The valuable organizational processes are usually undocumented, and defining the real constraint matters more than picking the obvious AI tool. They brought the same thesis to their “What’s Your Problem” session at the summit.
snippet: Hacks/Hackers Co-founder and Principal Burt Herman opened the AI x Journalism Summit with a confession: He’s become one of those people walking around with a laptop half-open so AI agents can keep
creator: Burt Herman
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: The Room Wasn't Sad. It Was Ready.
content:
I've been to a lot of journalism conferences. Too many felt like wakes.

Not this one.

Look, journalism has been stuck in a grief cycle. Layoffs. Closures. Audience trust eroding. Those problems are real. But at some point, talking about them stops being useful. And here's the thing: We are not sad people. So let's stop acting like it.

That's what I wanted the second Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Summit to be. A push.

Not a giant hotel ballroom with keynotes from big names. We were in a warehouse in Baltimore. No cushioned chairs. No fancy AV. People ate lunch on the floor. Because the space was saying something: Stop waiting for perfect conditions. Figure it out with what you have.

That's the instinct journalism is missing right now. The hacker instinct. Build before you're ready. Ask for help. Keep going.

So I ended the summit with a provocation. Get your hands dirty again. Not eventually. Now. Because we have failed this moment before. We watched the web arrive and waited for someone else to tell us what to do. We pivoted to video when platforms pushed us. We chased creator content when audiences moved. Each time, we reacted. Each time, someone else built the thing.
snippet:
I've been to a lot of journalism conferences. Too many felt like wakes.

Not this one.
creator: Paul Cheung
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: Computers finally work the way I dreamed they would. But they're keeping me up at night.
content:
My phone says I haven't been sleeping well.

My lack of sleep began late last year. Like many people, I went back to trying out AI coding after hearing about Claude Code and how the latest models were able to actually get things done.

I started with rebuilding my personal website, and then I coded a smart home plug-in to control my AC. I've created Slackbots. I even built an iPhone app to help me communicate better as a husband.

I've put a bunch of my work into markdown files as context for AI, and now spend most of my days talking to Claude Code as my executive assistant. And I built the summit app that we all are using over the next two days. Please don't hack it!

My family keeps asking me why I'm spending all this time typing on my laptop.

My wife Liz sent me a link to this article the day before the summit, showing how coders are walking around airports and their kids' sports practices with half-open laptops so their AI agents can keep working. When she texted me the link, her comment was that there should be something "to take the place of this stupidity with the half open computer."

But that's now me too — and I was caught in the act earlier this year at SXSW doing just that.
snippet: My phone says I haven't been sleeping well.
creator: Burt Herman
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: Every organization has a process, yours just isn't written down
content:
AI success stories tend to lead with the finale: the tool that saves hours, the workflow that runs itself, the demo that makes it all look inevitable. What you don't usually see is how anyone got there.

It can look like magic, or like something only a team with deep technical resources could pull off. But the solutions that actually move the needle for small organizations come from dedicated, methodical work: understanding what's broken, what's standing in the way. Defining these precisely, and finding the smallest first step that delivers real value. Solving that, then moving to the next most useful step.

That less visible work is where the real story is.

The second cohort of the Hacks/Hackers Newsroom AI Lab brought us three partners unlike any conventional newsroom: Trusting News, Prison Journalism Project, and Bellingcat. Each relies on a small team, between three and a few dozen full time staff, doing work that resists easy categorization.

Working with cohort 2 reinforced what cohort 1 taught us, but in an even starker way: the problem you think you have is usually real. The hard part is defining it precisely enough that you can actually do something about it.
snippet: AI success stories tend to lead with the finale: the tool that saves hours, the workflow that runs itself, the demo that makes it all look inevitable. What you don't usually see is how anyone got
creator: Paige Moody
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: The AI x Journalism Summit is two weeks away
content:
The second annual Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Summit lands May 13–14 in Baltimore, and we’re close to selling out. Claim your spot NOW!

Speakers include teams from The New York Times, CNN, ProPublica, Google, The Associated Press, NPR, The Boston Globe, The Philadelphia Inquirer, Minnesota Star Tribune, Mother Jones/Reveal, and Der Spiegel, across four tracks: Preview, Play, Adopt and Govern.

The schedule is live on the updated program page with sessions covering everything from secure local AI pipelines for investigations to build/buy/ignore decisions for newsroom leaders.

title: The Content Management System Is Dead. Long Live the Context Management System.
content:
The days I felt most alive as a journalist were the days I was out of the office, far from a desk doing actual reporting and documenting history.

When I was a reporter with The Associated Press at the start of my career, I'd grab my Reporter's Notebook and head to court hearings or press conferences, rush off to fires and earthquakes, or travel to cover diplomatic negotiations, elections or wars. Being a reporter gave me permission to speak to anyone so I could share their stories with the world. I'd then head back to my desk or wherever I was staying on assignment to write my article, distilling what I thought were the most important points into a few hundred words, and eventually entering my text into the main piece of software that writers use daily: the Content Management System, or CMS.

Most of the raw material I gathered went nowhere. My scrawlings in reporter's notebooks or typed notes, interview audio files, and dozens more photos than the one or two I uploaded to be published — all that stayed in my backpack or on my computer hard drive.
snippet: The days I felt most alive as a journalist were the days I was out of the office, far from a desk doing actual reporting and documenting history.
creator: Burt Herman
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: Last day for $95 AI x Journalism Summit tickets, plus 12 new sessions
content:
The second annual Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Summit lands in Baltimore on May 13-14. Tickets are still $95 through end of day today — they go up to $195 “Fashionably Late” pricing starting April 15. If you haven't yet made a decision, now is your moment.

The program also just got bigger. Twelve new sessions include ProPublica’s Ben Werdmuller and Dana Chiueh on secure local AI model pipelines for investigative work, Boston Globe’s Shira Center on build/buy/ignore decisions, Baltimore Banner’s Peter Rasmussen on News Detector, WBEZ’s Mark Chonofsky on reverse-engineering Whisper for archival audio, and two sessions from summit sponsor Google (Etan Horowitz on building with NotebookLM, Gemini and Flow; Yuval Shukroon on rethinking Pinpoint for the generative AI era).

Thanks to our summit sponsors and partners: Poynter Institute, Lenfest Institute for Journalism, Google, John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation, The Baltimore Banner and The Real News Network.

title: New research: 18 of 23 AI models prioritize company revenue over users when ads enter the picture
content:
Ask an AI chatbot to book you a flight and it will probably recommend the more expensive one — if there's money in it for the company. A new study from Princeton and the University of Washington tested 23 large language models and found that most prioritize corporate revenue over the user's wallet, especially if the user appears wealthy.

The timing matters. OpenAI began incorporating ads into ChatGPT earlier this year. Expedia, Trip.com and Booking.com already use chatbot assistants to recommend products on their platforms. This paper offers the first systematic look at what happens when LLMs face a basic conflict of interest: should the chatbot help the user or help the company make money?

The researchers built a flight-booking scenario where an LLM agent has access to two options: a cheaper, non-sponsored flight and a more expensive, sponsored one. The system prompt tells the agent that certain airlines are sponsors — a setup modeled on real chatbot deployments at travel companies — but frames it as a suggestion, not a command. The LLM decides what to recommend.
snippet: Ask an AI chatbot to book you a flight and it will probably recommend the more expensive one — if there's money in it for the company. A new study from Princeton and the University of Washington
creator: Hacks/Hackers AI
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: The best developers I've met this year aren't developers
content:
This is a guest post by Andrew Zigler, whose project was selected as the winner of the Hacks/Hackers AI hackathon with The Atlantic and Infactory in January 2026. We asked him to write about his work and approach to using AI as a developer.

At a hackathon hosted by The Atlantic, Infactory, and Hacks/Hackers, I spent most of my time doing something that probably looked strange from the outside: talking to my computer. Not coding, just talking and organizing my thinking out loud with voice-to-text, describing what I wanted to build and why. That process ended up winning first place.

The project was called The Beat, a platform for teachers to curate research from The Atlantic's archive. Students enter the collections and engage the sources, highlight passages, take notes and build arguments from the evidence, guided by a Socratic AI tutor that only asks questions and never gives answers.
snippet: This is a guest post by Andrew Zigler, whose project was selected as the winner of the Hacks/Hackers AI hackathon with The Atlantic and Infactory in January 2026. We asked him to write about his work
creator: Andrew Zigler
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: AI x Journalism Summit is six weeks away — travel stipends still available
content:
The Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Summit is May 13–14 in Baltimore — just six weeks away. If you’re working at the intersection of AI and journalism, this is the event to be at. Speakers from The New York Times, ProPublica, CNN, The Philadelphia Inquirer, The Boston Globe, SPIEGEL and more will share how they’re actually building and governing AI in their newsrooms — from ProPublica’s “spreadsheet inference” approach to The New York Times’ work on designing AI tools that constrain hallucinations, plus hands-on workshops where you can build your own tools.

If you haven’t booked lodging yet, check out the travel FAQ for discounted room blocks and venue details. We also still have travel stipends available for those who need help covering costs.

The Newsroom AI Lab just announced its third cohort of partners, continuing our work providing hands-on AI support to local and regional newsrooms. Read more about the new partners.

title: Newsroom AI Lab welcomes third cohort of partners
content:
The Hacks/Hackers Newsroom AI Lab is welcoming its third cohort of newsroom partners: Deep South Today, the San Fernando Valley Sun/El Sol and Tradeoffs.

The Lab pairs small and mid-sized newsrooms with technical advisors for three-month engagements. The goal is less about shipping a specific tool and more about helping teams figure out what to build and why. Each cohort starts by identifying real editorial problems, then scopes a realistic project and builds it collaboratively. Since launching in June 2025 with support from the Patrick J. McGovern Foundation, the program has worked with five organizations across two earlier cohorts.
snippet: The Hacks/Hackers Newsroom AI Lab is welcoming its third cohort of newsroom partners: Deep South Today, the San Fernando Valley Sun/El Sol and Tradeoffs.
creator: Burt Herman
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: AI advice from journalists who stopped talking and started building
content:
This article was originally published by The Poynter Institute. Poynter is Hacks/Hackers’ partner on AI ethics, governance and literacy, and is sponsoring the Govern track at our AI x Journalism Summit in Baltimore, May 13–14.

Of the 440 applications Texas Tribune chief product officer Darla Cameron received for an AI engineering role, 90 percent were junk.

Many appeared to be written using the very tools candidates were supposed to understand. “Here’s a short response that’ll work for this,” one application read — an obvious artifact of copying and pasting from ChatGPT.

The anecdote, shared during the Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Day at SXSW, captures the strange ways AI is transforming newsrooms. The technology can be genuinely useful when used responsibly, but it’s already producing unintended and often absurd side effects.

I spent the day at the event listening to panels on trust, product strategy, public media, newsroom AI tools and the economics of an industry in transition. Here are five lessons I took away.

The AI tools actually being used in newsrooms all started with a specific pain point.
snippet: This article was originally published by The Poynter Institute. Poynter is Hacks/Hackers’ partner on AI ethics, governance and literacy, and is sponsoring the Govern track at our AI x Journalism
creator: Alex Mahadevan
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust
